title: High-Performance Memory Management
subtitle: Stream the firehose without OOM — generators, lazy iterators, __slots__, and zero-copy buffers that process inputs larger than RAM.
goal: Process unbounded or out-of-core inputs in constant memory: replace materialized lists with generator pipelines, shrink per-object footprint with __slots__ and array, parse multi-gigabyte streams chunk-by-chunk, and move bytes without copying via memoryview.

blocks.heading: A generator is constant memory; a list is O(n) memory
blocks.html:
The single most important memory lever in Python is the difference between materializing a sequence and streaming it. [x*x for x in range(10**8)] allocates ~800 MB of int objects and pointers up front and will OOM a small container. (x*x for x in range(10**8)) allocates one generator frame — a few hundred bytes — and produces each value on demand, holding exactly one at a time.

The mechanism: a generator function compiles to a code object with CO_GENERATOR set. Calling it does not run the body; it returns a generator object whose frame is suspended at the top. Each next() resumes the frame until the next yield, then re-suspends, preserving locals on the heap. There is no intermediate container — values flow through.

The trade is real: a list is re-iterable, indexable, and len()-able; a generator is single-pass and exhausts. In a feed handler you almost always want single-pass streaming — you read each tick once and never look back. Reach for a list only when you genuinely need random access or multiple passes.

blocks.caption: Measuring the footprint gap — list vs generator
blocks.code:
import sys

# A list comprehension materializes every element + the list's own array.
lst = [x * x for x in range(100_000)]
print("list object:", sys.getsizeof(lst), "bytes (just the pointer array)")

# The generator holds one suspended frame, regardless of length.
gen = (x * x for x in range(100_000))
print("generator object:", sys.getsizeof(gen), "bytes")

# Consuming lazily: sum never builds a container.
print("sum via generator:", sum(x * x for x in range(100_000)))
blocks.explain: sys.getsizeof on the list reports only its internal pointer array (~800KB here) and excludes the 100k int objects it references — the true cost is far larger. The generator is a fixed ~100-200 bytes no matter the length. sum(genexpr) is the canonical constant-memory reduction: no list is ever built.

blocks.html: Genexpr vs list-comp at the call site. When a function consumes an iterable once, drop the brackets: sum([x for x in it]) builds a throwaway list first; sum(x for x in it) streams. Same for any, all, min, max, "".join(...), and set(...). The one exception: sorted and len must materialize anyway, so brackets cost nothing there.

blocks.heading: itertools is the lazy standard library — combinators that never materialize
blocks.html:
itertools gives you composable, C-speed iterator transforms that hold no backing buffer. The ones you reach for constantly under memory pressure:

  • islice(it, start, stop, step) — lazy slicing; the only safe way to take the first k of an infinite or huge iterator.
  • chain(*its) — concatenate iterables without copying them into one list.
  • groupby(it, key) — group consecutive equal keys (requires pre-sorted input), streaming.
  • accumulate(it, func) — running fold (prefix sums, running max) one element at a time.
  • tee(it, n) — fork one iterator into n — but beware: it buffers everything consumed by the fastest branch, so it is not free.

The discipline: build a pipeline of generators where each stage pulls from the previous. Memory stays flat because only one element is in flight per stage, no matter how many stages or how large the source.

blocks.caption: A generator pipeline: parse → filter → transform → reduce, all lazy
blocks.code:
import itertools as it

def read_ticks(lines):
    for line in lines:                      # source: one line at a time
        sym, px = line.split(",")
        yield sym, float(px)

def only(symbol, ticks):
    for sym, px in ticks:                   # filter stage
        if sym == symbol:
            yield px

raw = ["AAPL,191.2", "MSFT,420.0", "AAPL,191.5", "AAPL,190.9", "NVDA,1200.0"]

# Compose stages. Nothing runs until the final reduction pulls.
prices = only("AAPL", read_ticks(raw))
running_max = it.accumulate(prices, max)    # lazy running max
print(list(running_max))                     # [191.2, 191.5, 191.5]
blocks.explain: Each stage is a generator that pulls one item from the stage upstream. read_ticks never holds more than one parsed tick; only() forwards matches; accumulate folds a running max. Swap `raw` for a 50GB file object and the memory profile is identical — flat. This is the streaming-ETL shape for out-of-core data.

blocks.heading: __slots__: kill the per-instance __dict__
blocks.html:
By default every instance carries a __dict__ (a hash table) to hold its attributes. For one object that's fine; for ten million order records it is the dominant cost — each __dict__ is hundreds of bytes of overhead before your data.

Declaring __slots__ = ("price", "qty") tells Python to store those attributes in a fixed C-level array on the instance instead of a dict. The instance loses its __dict__ (you can no longer add arbitrary attributes), and footprint drops by roughly 50–60% for small objects. Attribute access also gets slightly faster (slot descriptor vs dict lookup).

Costs to know: no dynamic attributes, multiple-inheritance with slots needs care, and a slotted class can't have a class-level default for a slot name. For the millions-of-small-records case that defines HFT data structures, it is almost always the right call — or skip the class entirely and use a parallel array.array / columnar layout.

blocks.caption: __slots__ vs __dict__ footprint, and array vs object-list
blocks.code:
import sys

class Order:                       # default: has a per-instance __dict__
    def __init__(self, px, qty):
        self.px = px
        self.qty = qty

class SlotOrder:
    __slots__ = ("px", "qty")      # fixed C array, no __dict__
    def __init__(self, px, qty):
        self.px = px
        self.qty = qty

a = Order(101, 5)
b = SlotOrder(101, 5)
print("dict-based instance:", sys.getsizeof(a) + sys.getsizeof(a.__dict__))
print("slotted instance:   ", sys.getsizeof(b))

import array
# Columnar: a million prices as a C double array, not a million float objects.
col = array.array("d", (float(i) for i in range(1000)))
print("array of 1000 doubles:", sys.getsizeof(col), "bytes (~8 each + header)")
blocks.explain: The dict-based instance pays for both the object header AND its separate __dict__ hash table. The slotted instance stores px/qty inline — typically half the bytes. For purely numeric columns, array.array beats even slots: it stores raw C doubles contiguously with zero per-element Python-object overhead, the columnar layout real tick stores use.

blocks.html: __slots__ pitfalls. A subclass that does not define __slots__ silently regains a __dict__, erasing the savings — every class in the hierarchy must declare slots. And you cannot weakref a slotted instance unless you add "__weakref__" to the slots. Measure with sys.getsizeof before assuming you won the bytes back.

blocks.heading: Streaming parsers: process inputs larger than RAM
blocks.html:
The rule for out-of-core data: never call .read() or .readlines() on a huge source. Both pull the entire payload into memory. Instead iterate the file object directly (for line in f: yields one line at a time via buffered I/O) or pull fixed-size chunks with iter(lambda: f.read(CHUNK), b"").

The iter(callable, sentinel) two-argument form is the canonical chunked-read idiom: it calls the zero-arg callable repeatedly until it returns the sentinel, yielding each result. With f.read(65536) as the callable and b"" as the sentinel, you stream a file in 64 KB blocks in constant memory — even if the file is a terabyte.

The subtlety with fixed chunks is record boundaries: a logical record (a line, a length-prefixed frame) can straddle two chunks. The streaming-parser pattern keeps a small carry buffer of the unfinished tail, prepends it to the next chunk, and emits only complete records. Memory stays bounded by the largest single record, not the file.

blocks.caption: Chunked reader with carry buffer — emit complete lines without read()
blocks.code:
import io

def stream_lines(stream, chunk_size=8):
    """Yield complete '\n'-terminated lines from a binary stream,
    never holding more than one chunk + one partial line in memory."""
    carry = b""
    for block in iter(lambda: stream.read(chunk_size), b""):
        carry += block
        while b"\n" in carry:
            line, carry = carry.split(b"\n", 1)   # emit one complete record
            yield line.decode()
    if carry:                                       # trailing line, no newline
        yield carry.decode()

src = io.BytesIO(b"AAPL,191\nMSFT,420\nNVDA,1200\nAMD,160")
print(list(stream_lines(src, chunk_size=8)))
blocks.explain: iter(lambda: stream.read(8), b"") is the two-arg-iter chunked-read idiom: pull 8 bytes at a time until EOF (b""). The `carry` buffer holds the partial line that straddles a chunk boundary; we only yield once a full '\n'-delimited record is assembled. Peak memory is one chunk plus one line — independent of total stream size.

blocks.heading: memoryview & bytearray: slice bytes without copying
blocks.html:
Slicing bytes or bytearray copies: buf[10:1_000_000] allocates a ~1 MB duplicate. In a hot parse loop over a large frame, those copies dominate. A memoryview is a zero-copy window onto the same underlying buffer — slicing a memoryview produces another view, no bytes moved.

This is the buffer-protocol contract: memoryview(buf)[a:b] shares storage with buf. You can read through it, and if the source is writable (a bytearray), write through it too — edits land in the original. Combine with bytearray for an in-place mutable byte buffer you parse and patch without reallocating.

The practical win: parse a length-prefixed protocol by walking an offset and taking mv[offset:offset+n] views for each field — you decode fields touching zero extra allocation until you actually materialize a value. Release views with .release() (or a with block) before resizing the source.

blocks.caption: Zero-copy field extraction from a length-prefixed frame
blocks.code:
# Frame layout: [1-byte length][payload] repeated.
frame = bytearray(b"\x03abc\x05hello\x02hi")
mv = memoryview(frame)

offset = 0
fields = []
while offset < len(mv):
    n = mv[offset]                 # length byte (int)
    offset += 1
    view = mv[offset:offset + n]   # zero-copy view of the payload
    fields.append(bytes(view))     # materialize only when we keep it
    offset += n
print([f.decode() for f in fields])

# Writing through a view mutates the original buffer in place (no realloc):
mv2 = memoryview(frame)
mv2[1:4] = b"XYZ"
print(frame[:4])                   # bytearray(b'\x03XYZ')
blocks.explain: mv[offset:offset+n] never copies — it's a window into `frame`. We only pay for bytes when we explicitly call bytes(view) to retain a field. Writing mv2[1:4] = b'XYZ' edits the shared buffer in place. This is how you parse multi-MB frames with allocation proportional to fields kept, not frame size.

blocks.question: You write `g = (x*x for x in range(1_000_000))`, then call `list(g)` and immediately `sum(g)`. What does sum return?
options: 0 — the generator was exhausted by list(g) and yields nothing
It re-runs the generator from the start
blocks.explain: Generators are single-pass. list(g) drains it to exhaustion; the second consumer (sum) sees an empty iterator and returns 0. To iterate twice you must rebuild the generator or materialize once into a list (paying the memory).

blocks.question: Which slice operation does NOT allocate a copy of the underlying bytes?
blocks.explain: Slicing bytes/bytearray copies, and split builds new bytes objects. Only memoryview slicing returns another view that shares the original storage — zero copy. That's the whole point of memoryview in a hot parse path.

blocks.question: You add `__slots__ = ('a', 'b')` to a class to save memory on millions of instances, but footprint barely changes. The most likely cause?
options: A subclass without its own __slots__ reintroduced a per-instance __dict__
__slots__ only affects class attributes, not instances
blocks.explain: If any class in the hierarchy omits __slots__, instances regain a __dict__ and the savings vanish. Every class in the MRO must declare __slots__ for the optimization to hold.

blocks.title: Constant-memory sum of squares
blocks.instructions: Reduce a huge range to the sum of squares WITHOUT building any list. Replace the TODO with a single generator-expression reduction so memory stays flat. Print the result for n=10000.
blocks.starter:
def sum_squares(n):
    # TODO: return the sum of i*i for i in range(n) WITHOUT materializing a list
    raise NotImplementedError

print(sum_squares(10000))
blocks.passMsg: Flat memory: sum(genexpr) folds one square at a time — no list is ever allocated.
blocks.failMsg: Use sum(i*i for i in range(n)) — a generator expression, not a list comprehension, so nothing is materialized.

blocks.title: Lazy first-k with islice
blocks.instructions: You have a potentially infinite source. Take only the first 5 values lazily — never consuming more than needed — and print them as a list. Use itertools.islice.
blocks.starter:
import itertools

def naturals():
    i = 0
    while True:
        yield i
        i += 1

# TODO: take the first 5 values from naturals() lazily, as a list
result = None
print(result)
blocks.solution:
import itertools

def naturals():
    i = 0
    while True:
        yield i
        i += 1

result = list(itertools.islice(naturals(), 5))
print(result)
blocks.passMsg: islice pulls exactly 5 items then stops — the only safe way to slice an infinite iterator.
blocks.failMsg: Use list(itertools.islice(naturals(), 5)) — plain slicing [:5] doesn't work on a generator.

problems.title: Streaming top-K by frequency in one pass, bounded memory
problems.instructions:
A symbol firehose is too large to materialize. Read it as a stream and report the K most frequently seen symbols using a single pass and memory proportional to the number of distinct symbols only (a streaming counter), never the stream length.

Stdin format: line 1 = one int K. Each subsequent line is one symbol token (a non-empty string, no spaces). The stream ends at EOF. Treat the lines as a stream — do not assume you may load them all (your solution should iterate, not index).

Stdout: the top K symbols by descending count; ties broken by ascending symbol (lexicographic). Print one per line as symbol count. If fewer than K distinct symbols exist, print all of them.

This is the canonical streaming-aggregation shape: a fixed-size dict of counts grows only with cardinality, and you sort the small distinct set at the end — not the unbounded stream.
problems.starter:
import sys

def main():
    it = iter(sys.stdin)
    k = int(next(it).strip())
    # Stream the rest: accumulate counts in a dict keyed by symbol.
    # Memory is O(distinct symbols), NOT O(stream length).
    # TODO: for each remaining line (a symbol), increment its count.
    # TODO: select the top K by (-count, symbol) and print "symbol count".
    raise NotImplementedError

main()
problems.solution:
import sys

def main():
    it = iter(sys.stdin)
    k = int(next(it).strip())
    counts = {}
    for line in it:                       # single streaming pass
        sym = line.strip()
        if not sym:
            continue
        counts[sym] = counts.get(sym, 0) + 1
    # Sort only the distinct set: descending count, then ascending symbol.
    ranked = sorted(counts.items(), key=lambda kv: (-kv[1], kv[0]))
    out = []
    for sym, c in ranked[:k]:
        out.append(f"{sym} {c}")
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))
    if out:
        sys.stdout.write("\n")

main()
problems.stdin:
2
AAPL
MSFT
AAPL
NVDA
AAPL
MSFT

tests.name: basic top-2
tests.stdin:
2
AAPL
MSFT
AAPL
NVDA
AAPL
MSFT

tests.name: tie broken lexicographically
tests.stdin:
3
b
a
c
a
b
c

complexity.codeTemplate:
import random
random.seed(11)
N = {N}
# Streaming counter over N tokens drawn from a fixed-cardinality alphabet.
# One dict pass is O(N); final sort is over the small distinct set.
counts = {}
for _ in range(N):
    sym = str(random.randint(0, 255))
    counts[sym] = counts.get(sym, 0) + 1
ranked = sorted(counts.items(), key=lambda kv: (-kv[1], kv[0]))
print(ranked[0][0])

problems.hint: Iterate sys.stdin line by line (it's already a lazy iterator) and keep a dict of counts — that dict is the only thing that grows, and only with distinct symbols. At the end, sort counts.items() by key=lambda kv: (-kv[1], kv[0]) and slice [:K].

problems.title: Chunked record framing across buffer boundaries
problems.instructions:
You receive a byte stream of length-prefixed records: each record is one ASCII-digit length token, a single space, then exactly that many payload bytes — concatenated with no separators between records. The catch: in production these arrive in fixed-size network chunks and a record can straddle a chunk boundary, so you must parse with a carry buffer that holds the unfinished tail.

Stdin format: line 1 = one int CHUNK (the chunk size to simulate). Line 2 = the raw stream as a single string (it contains no newlines; spaces are part of the framing). Read line 2, encode it to bytes, and feed it to your parser CHUNK bytes at a time — you must not parse the whole buffer at once; carry partial records between chunks.

Example stream 3 abc5 hello2 hi decodes to records abc, hello, hi. (After a length token's space, the next len bytes are payload; immediately after, the next length token begins.)

Stdout: each decoded payload on its own line, in order. Final line: records=<count>.
problems.starter:
import sys

def parse_stream(chunks):
    """chunks: an iterable of bytes blocks. Yield decoded payload strings."""
    carry = b""
    # State machine: read a length token up to the space, then `length`
    # payload bytes. Anything incomplete stays in `carry` for the next chunk.
    # TODO: implement framing across chunk boundaries and yield payloads.
    raise NotImplementedError

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split("\n")
    chunk = int(data[0])
    stream = data[1].encode() if len(data) > 1 else b""
    chunks = (stream[i:i + chunk] for i in range(0, len(stream), chunk))
    count = 0
    out = []
    for payload in parse_stream(chunks):
        out.append(payload)
        count += 1
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))
    if out:
        sys.stdout.write("\n")
    sys.stdout.write(f"records={count}\n")

main()
problems.solution:
import sys

def parse_stream(chunks):
    carry = b""
    for block in chunks:
        carry += block
        while True:
            sp = carry.find(b" ")
            if sp == -1:
                break                      # length token not fully arrived
            length = int(carry[:sp])
            start = sp + 1
            end = start + length
            if len(carry) < end:
                break                      # payload not fully arrived yet
            yield carry[start:end].decode()
            carry = carry[end:]            # drop the consumed record
    # any leftover carry is an incomplete trailing record -> ignored

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split("\n")
    chunk = int(data[0])
    stream = data[1].encode() if len(data) > 1 else b""
    chunks = (stream[i:i + chunk] for i in range(0, len(stream), chunk))
    count = 0
    out = []
    for payload in parse_stream(chunks):
        out.append(payload)
        count += 1
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))
    if out:
        sys.stdout.write("\n")
    sys.stdout.write(f"records={count}\n")

main()

tests.name: payload contains spaces
tests.expectStdout:
a b c
hello world
records=2

problems.hint: Append each chunk to a carry buffer. Loop: find the first space to read the length token, then check whether carry already holds space+length payload bytes. If not, break and wait for the next chunk. After emitting, slice the consumed bytes off carry. Note payloads may themselves contain spaces — only the FIRST space after a length token is the delimiter.

problems.title: Zero-copy column extraction from a packed buffer
problems.instructions:
A fixed-width binary table is packed into one contiguous buffer: R rows, each row exactly W bytes. You must compute a per-column byte-sum over a single requested column range without copying row data out — walk the buffer with a memoryview and sum the bytes of the target slice of each row directly from the shared storage.

Stdin format: line 1 = four ints R W C0 C1 — rows, row width, and a half-open column range [C0, C1) within each row. Line 2 = R*W space-separated ints in 0..255, the buffer contents in row-major order.

Stdout: one line per row: the sum of bytes in that row's columns [C0, C1). Final line: total=<grand sum>.

Build the buffer as a bytearray, wrap it once in a memoryview, and take mv[row_start+C0 : row_start+C1] per row — those slices are views, not copies.
problems.starter:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    idx = 0
    r = int(data[idx]); idx += 1
    w = int(data[idx]); idx += 1
    c0 = int(data[idx]); idx += 1
    c1 = int(data[idx]); idx += 1
    buf = bytearray(int(data[idx + i]) for i in range(r * w)); idx += r * w
    mv = memoryview(buf)
    # TODO: for each row, sum bytes in columns [c0, c1) via a zero-copy view
    #       mv[row*w + c0 : row*w + c1]; print that sum per row.
    # TODO: print f"total=<grand sum>".
    raise NotImplementedError

main()
problems.solution:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    idx = 0
    r = int(data[idx]); idx += 1
    w = int(data[idx]); idx += 1
    c0 = int(data[idx]); idx += 1
    c1 = int(data[idx]); idx += 1
    buf = bytearray(int(data[idx + i]) for i in range(r * w)); idx += r * w
    mv = memoryview(buf)
    out = []
    total = 0
    for row in range(r):
        base = row * w
        s = sum(mv[base + c0:base + c1])   # zero-copy view; sum of ints
        out.append(str(s))
        total += s
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))
    if out:
        sys.stdout.write("\n")
    sys.stdout.write(f"total={total}\n")

main()
problems.hint: Wrap the bytearray once in memoryview(buf). For row r the byte offset is r*w; take mv[r*w + c0 : r*w + c1] and sum() it — iterating a memoryview of bytes yields ints, so sum() gives the byte total directly, no copy.

problems.title: Lazy windowed aggregation over a stream
problems.instructions:
Compute a sliding-window sum of width K over a numeric stream, emitting one output per window, using O(K) memory regardless of stream length. Use a generator that keeps only a fixed-size deque of the last K values plus a running total — never the whole input.

Stdin format: line 1 = two ints K M (window width, number of values). Line 2 = M space-separated ints, the stream.

Stdout: the window sums in order, one per line — there are M - K + 1 of them (the first is the sum of values[0..K-1]). If M < K, print nothing. Final line: windows=<count>.

This is the bounded-memory streaming aggregate: maintain the running sum incrementally (add the entering value, subtract the leaving one) so each window is O(1) and total memory is O(K).
problems.starter:
import sys
from collections import deque

def windowed_sums(values, k):
    """Yield sliding-window sums of width k using O(k) memory."""
    win = deque()
    total = 0
    # TODO: feed each value; once the window holds k items, yield the sum,
    #       then evict the oldest (subtract it) so memory stays O(k).
    raise NotImplementedError

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    k = int(data[0]); m = int(data[1])
    values = (int(data[2 + i]) for i in range(m))   # lazy source
    out = []
    for s in windowed_sums(values, k):
        out.append(str(s))
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))
    if out:
        sys.stdout.write("\n")
    sys.stdout.write(f"windows={len(out)}\n")

main()
problems.solution:
import sys
from collections import deque

def windowed_sums(values, k):
    win = deque()
    total = 0
    for v in values:
        win.append(v)
        total += v
        if len(win) == k:
            yield total
            total -= win.popleft()      # evict oldest, keep O(k)

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    k = int(data[0]); m = int(data[1])
    values = (int(data[2 + i]) for i in range(m))
    out = []
    for s in windowed_sums(values, k):
        out.append(str(s))
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))
    if out:
        sys.stdout.write("\n")
    sys.stdout.write(f"windows={len(out)}\n")

main()

complexity.codeTemplate:
from collections import deque
N = {N}
K = 64
win = deque()
total = 0
last = 0
for v in range(N):
    win.append(v)
    total += v
    if len(win) == K:
        last = total
        total -= win.popleft()
print(last)

problems.hint: Keep a deque and a running total. Append+add each value; when the deque hits K, yield the total, then popleft and subtract it. That makes each window O(1) and total memory O(K) — independent of M.

problems.title: Memory-frugal aggregation: __slots__ columnar group-by
problems.instructions:
Aggregate trades by symbol into compact accumulator objects. To keep per-group footprint minimal at scale you must use a class with __slots__ (no per-instance __dict__) as the accumulator — tracking total quantity and notional (price×qty) per symbol.

Stdin format: line 1 = one int N (number of trades). Next N lines: symbol price qty where price and qty are non-negative integers and symbol has no spaces.

Stdout: one line per symbol, sorted ascending by symbol: symbol total_qty notional where notional = sum(price*qty) over that symbol's trades. Final line: symbols=<distinct count>.

Your accumulator class MUST declare __slots__. The grader checks output correctness; the constraint trains the millions-of-small-objects footprint discipline where __slots__ is mandatory.
problems.starter:
import sys

class Agg:
    # TODO: declare __slots__ for the accumulated fields (no __dict__).
    def __init__(self):
        self.qty = 0
        self.notional = 0

def main():
    it = iter(sys.stdin)
    n = int(next(it).strip())
    groups = {}
    for _ in range(n):
        sym, price, qty = next(it).split()
        price = int(price); qty = int(qty)
        # TODO: fetch-or-create the Agg for sym and accumulate qty + price*qty
        raise NotImplementedError
    # TODO: print "symbol total_qty notional" sorted by symbol, then count.

main()
problems.solution:
import sys

class Agg:
    __slots__ = ("qty", "notional")
    def __init__(self):
        self.qty = 0
        self.notional = 0

def main():
    it = iter(sys.stdin)
    n = int(next(it).strip())
    groups = {}
    for _ in range(n):
        sym, price, qty = next(it).split()
        price = int(price); qty = int(qty)
        a = groups.get(sym)
        if a is None:
            a = Agg()
            groups[sym] = a
        a.qty += qty
        a.notional += price * qty
    out = []
    for sym in sorted(groups):
        a = groups[sym]
        out.append(f"{sym} {a.qty} {a.notional}")
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))
    if out:
        sys.stdout.write("\n")
    sys.stdout.write(f"symbols={len(groups)}\n")

main()

tests.name: zero qty contributes nothing to notional

problems.hint: Give Agg `__slots__ = ('qty', 'notional')` so instances carry no __dict__. Use groups.get(sym) to fetch-or-create the accumulator, then add qty and price*qty. Sort the dict keys at the end and emit one line per symbol.
